Software Engineering

Cloud Computing

Artificial Intelligence

Technology

Programming

5 min read
Just now

--

Fifteen years is a long time to keep everything you know inside your head.
I started as a software engineer in 2011. Since then, I have led the architecture of enterprise software solutions deployed across UAE, Qatar, KSA, Oman and Kuwait. I have built and scaled teams of 30+ engineers. I have integrated AI into core business workflows before most organizations in the region knew what a language model was. I have navigated health authority compliance simultaneously across multiple client deployments. I have designed microservices architectures on Azure & AWS, built AI Hub features with Semantic Kernel and LangChain, and operated Oracle RAC systems at enterprise scale.
And in fifteen years, I never wrote a single word about any of it publicly.
Not because I had nothing to say. But because I was always focused on delivering — the next release, the next client, the next architecture decision.
Recently, I stopped and asked myself a question I should have asked much earlier: what happens to everything I know if I never write it down?
The answer made me uncomfortable. And it made me start writing.
The Trap Every Senior Engineer Falls Into
There is a trap that experienced engineers fall into — and almost nobody names it.
The longer you stay in your domain, the deeper your expertise becomes. But depth without visibility has a hidden cost. Everything you know about your industry, your architecture, your hard-won production lessons — it stays locked in your head, shared only with the people who sit near you.
You accumulate years of knowledge: the architecture decisions that failed at scale, the regulatory nuances that no documentation explains cleanly, the AI integration patterns that work in production versus the ones that only look good in a demo. You carry all of this as institutional memory.
But to the broader technology community? It does not exist.
I realized this while reflecting on the pace of change happening across the GCC technology market — watching enterprises transform, watching AI reshape how software is built and operated — and something clicked. The knowledge I had built over fifteen years was not reaching anyone beyond my immediate organization.
I had spent fifteen years building expertise and zero time making it useful to the wider community. That needed to change.
Why the GCC Needs More Senior Technical Voices
This is not just about me. There is something bigger happening here that motivated me to start.
The GCC technology market is one of the most dynamic in the world right now. The UAE AI Strategy 2031. Saudi Arabia’s Vision 2030. Qatar’s National AI Strategy. Billions of dirhams invested in cloud infrastructure, AI systems, and digital transformation across the region — and the pace is accelerating.
And yet, when you search for technical content specific to this market — Azure architecture for UAE data residency requirements, enterprise microservices patterns for GCC compliance, AI engineering for Arabic-language workflows, DevOps practices for multi-country deployments across the Gulf — almost nothing exists.
Most technical content is written for Silicon Valley companies, Western enterprise contexts, and use cases that do not map cleanly to the GCC. The regulatory environment here is different. The enterprise architecture constraints are different. The talent market is different. The compliance landscape is different.
Senior engineers who have actually built production systems in this region — who have navigated real GCC regulatory requirements, multi-country deployments, and enterprise cloud architecture — are not writing. They are delivering. Just like I was.
Someone needs to start writing this down.
I decided it should be me.
What I Will Write About
I will not write generic tutorials you can find anywhere. My focus is specific, production-grade, and specifically GCC-relevant.
Cloud Architecture for GCC Enterprises. Azure, AWS, microservices, multi-region resilience, and compliance-aware design for the UAE and Gulf market. Real architectural decisions from real enterprise systems — not textbook theory.
Agentic AI and LLM Engineering. I am actively building AI-powered systems using LangChain, LangGraph, RAG pipelines, and MCP servers. I will write about what works in production, what fails at scale, and what the current hype gets wrong.
DevOps at Enterprise Scale. Docker, Kubernetes, AKS, Helm, ArgoCD, Terraform — building and operating production-grade delivery pipelines for large engineering organizations in the region.
Engineering Leadership. What it actually takes to lead 30+ engineers across three countries. How to scale a team without losing technical depth. How to build engineering culture in a market where talent is scarce and expectations are rapidly evolving.
Engineering Culture and Team Building. How to grow engineers, not just manage them. What separates teams that consistently deliver from teams that consistently plan to deliver.
Every piece I write will come from real production experience in the GCC — not conference slides, not vendor documentation, not recycled Western frameworks applied without context.
Who This Is For
If you are a senior engineer, engineering manager, architect, or technology leader working in the GCC — this is for you.
If you are building enterprise systems on Azure or AWS in the UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, or Saudi Arabia — this is for you.
If you are navigating the complexity of GCC compliance, multi-country deployments, or enterprise AI integration — you already know how little useful content exists for your context. This is for you.
If you are a senior engineer who has spent years delivering and zero time sharing — you know exactly what I am talking about. This is for you too.
A Final Thought
The best engineers I have worked with are not the ones who know the most. They are the ones who make their knowledge useful to others — through the systems they build, the teams they develop, and increasingly, through what they write.
The GCC technology community deserves more senior technical voices writing from real production experience. Not from the outside looking in — but from fifteen years of actually building here, shipping here, and solving problems that are specific to this market and this region.
If you are building enterprise technology in the Gulf — follow along. I promise to make it worth your time.
Connect on LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/shanavas-a-samad
